metadata.title: The Electoral College, explained (excerpt recreation)
metadata.purpose: TEST FIXTURE for aspects/explainer/vox-explainer — ~110s excerpt, every shot type exercised. Clock = source transcript timestamps (no TTS). Visual ground truth = ../../vox/ frames.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — transcript timestamps were the pass-1 stand-in

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated archival plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation rings used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, crimson vs dusty navy for partisan data, terracotta/yellow as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.note: Counts to verify: 435 House seats, 270 to win, 36 TX reps / 1 VT rep, 38/3 electoral votes, 3.5x WY vs CA, 21 vs 15 electoral votes VA/PA 1800, 347000/1700 enslaved, 539000/601000 free, Gossett quote 1950 (Keyssar).
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B22: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. If you watch the news during a presidential election, you'll hear a lot of this:

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] The Electoral College, explained

beats.narration_text: "What do the national polls look like?" "Hillary Clinton's national lead..."
beats.scene_description: Cable-news montage: anchors over election-night graphics, treated to newsprint desaturation, stacked as two overlapping collage panels
beats.image_prompt: two overlapping television-news still frames pinned on a newsprint page, anchors at desks with election graphics, desaturated print look, flat collage
beats.video_prompt: quick 2-cut montage of news broadcast clips, slight paper-plane parallax, no camera move within clips

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B02.png (or .mp4)
build.suggested: find: quick 2-cut montage of news broadcast clips, slight paper-plane parallax, no camera move within clips

beats.narration_text: "Nationally, Joe Biden is currently ahead."
beats.scene_description: third news panel slides onto the collage over the previous two
beats.video_prompt: one more broadcast panel drops onto the paper stack, settles flat

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B03.png (or .mp4)
build.suggested: find: one more broadcast panel drops onto the paper stack, settles flat

beats.narration_text: But these national polls don't actually tell you who's going to win.
beats.scene_description: the news panels get a single hand-drawn terracotta X struck through them on the annotation plane

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B04.png (or .mp4)
build.suggested: find: [ANNOTATE] terracotta strike-X over the polls

beats.narration_text: "Throw the national polls out the window, they don't matter at all. What matters are those swing states." Ah yes. The swing states.
beats.scene_description: pull-quote set large in charcoal serif on the newsprint ground; golden highlighter bar sweeps 'swing states' as it is spoken

document.quote: What matters are those swing states.

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B05.png (or .mp4)
build.suggested: find: [QUOTE] 'What matters are those swing states.'

beats.narration_text: The Electoral College is based on how people are represented in Congress:
beats.scene_description: archival photograph of the U.S. Capitol, desaturated, seated on the newsprint page with cream border; slow push-in
beats.image_prompt: archival black-and-white photograph of the United States Capitol dome, early 20th century, desaturated print reproduction on aged newsprint

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B06.png (or .mp4)

beats.narration_text: where each state has a number of representatives based on its population, and every state also gets two senators.
So, for example, let's look at Texas, which has a huge population, and Vermont, which has a really small one.
beats.scene_description: two slate-teal card blocks with white serif state names and state silhouettes: Texas large, Vermont small — matches vox_frame_009520 composition

beats.narration_text: Texas has 36 representatives in Congress. Vermont only gets one.
In the Electoral College, a state gets the same number of delegates as their Congressional representatives, plus two — for each senator. So Texas has 38 electoral votes. Vermont has 3.

production_viz.label: Electoral votes
production_viz.note: the +2 squares land in terracotta, timed to 'plus two'

beats.new_visual_element: [ISOTYPE] Electoral votes — TX 38 · VT 3 (+2 lands terracotta)

beats.narration_text: These are the results of the 2016 election, by state.

production_viz.label: 2016 election results by state
production_viz.source_data: MIT Election Lab / FEC 2016 state returns

beats.scene_description: 50-state stacked bar chart, crimson/navy/gray, serif state labels angled — matches vox_frame_004592

beats.narration_text: You're probably more familiar with this version of it: a map of red states, and blue states.
beats.scene_description: red/blue 2016 choropleth on newsprint — matches vox_frame_004816

beats.narration_text: But this chart tells a different story. You can see that no state is actually all-red or all-blue.

production_viz.note: yellow hand-drawn ellipse sweeps the band of blue bases under red tops — matches vox_frame_004928

annotation.target: the mixed midsection of every bar

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] yellow ellipse — no state is all-red or all-blue

beats.narration_text: But almost every state awards its electoral votes the same way: The candidate who gets the most votes in a state, gets ALL its electoral votes.
beats.scene_description: isotype square grid over newsprint; scattered red-outlined and blue-outlined regions consume the whole grid — matches vox_frame_004368

production_viz.label: winner-take-all
production_viz.note: outline regions flood to solid on 'ALL'

beats.narration_text: As a compromise, they settled on something called the "three-fifths clause."
beats.scene_description: scan of the Constitution's Article I text seated on newsprint; slow zoom toward the three-fifths passage; crimson label chip 'Three-fifths clause' drops in — chip matches vox_frame_009856

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B15.png (or .mp4)

beats.narration_text: It established that an enslaved person would only count as three-fifths of a person.
beats.scene_description: five isotype squares; three fill charcoal, the remaining two stay outlined — quiet, no flourish

production_viz.note: 3 of 5 fill; restraint: no other element on screen

beats.narration_text: In 1800, Pennsylvania, a northern state, and Virginia, a southern one, had about the same number of free people living there. But Virginia was also home to hundreds of thousands of enslaved people,
beats.scene_description: Virginia and Pennsylvania slate cards with serif figures below: Free people 539,000 / 601,000; then Enslaved people 347,000 / 1,700 in terracotta — matches vox_frame_009520 and _009072

beats.narration_text: who had no freedom, let alone a vote, and ended up with more votes in the Electoral College than Pennsylvania.
beats.scene_description: black square grids flank the cards: 21 beside Virginia (its Virginia card ringed in the three-fifths crimson frame), 15 beside Pennsylvania — matches vox_frame_009072

beats.narration_text: In the 1948 presidential election, New York ended up being the major swing state.
beats.scene_description: 1948 map: navy Truman, crimson Dewey, gold Thurmond; serif legend names underlined in their color; hand-drawn terracotta ring lands on New York — matches vox_frame_013440

beats.narration_text: A Congressman from Texas said, "I have no objection to the Negro in Harlem voting. But I do resent the fact that his vote is worth a hundred times as much as the vote of a white man in Texas."
beats.scene_description: full-frame serif pull-quote on newsprint, attribution '—Rep. Ed Gossett, Texas / 1950', 'Source: Alexander Keyssar' bottom-left — matches vox_frame_013888

document.quote: I have no objection to the Negro in Harlem voting ... but I do resent the fact that ... his vote is worth a hundred times as much ... as is the vote of a white man in Texas.

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B20.png (or .mp4)

beats.narration_text: Swing states change. What doesn't, is that the Electoral College gives certain people more power to pick the president.
beats.scene_description: archival photograph of voters at a polling place mid-century, desaturated on newsprint, very slow pull-out
beats.image_prompt: mid-20th-century archival photograph of American voters lined up at a polling place, desaturated newsprint reproduction

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B21.png (or .mp4)

beats.narration_text: And its biggest defenders have always been those who benefit the most from it.

card.copy: recreated as a vox-explainer test — not for publication
